title: MLOps & Productionization
pitchHtml: A model that wins on a validation set is a science result, not a product. MLOps is the discipline of turning that result into something that runs reliably, costs a predictable amount, and keeps working after the data shifts under it. This module is the leader's view of that discipline: not how to write the Dockerfile or the FastAPI handler — the ML Engineer Path does that — but what to budget, staff, monitor, and refuse to promise. The recurring question of this path takes a specific shape here: the value of a model is realised only in production, and the run cost and reliability tax of keeping it there is the part clients (and inexperienced teams) systematically forget to price.

items.html: Explain the "notebook → product" gap and why most models never ship — in business, not technical, terms
Choose a serving pattern (batch / real-time / streaming / edge) from a use case, and know its cost and reliability implications
Describe what monitoring and drift detection must catch, and tie the alerts back to evaluation metrics
Decide a retraining cadence and justify it on cost vs decay, not on habit
Explain how CI/CD for ML differs from software CI/CD (the extra data + model gates)
Scope LLMOps for a GenAI app: prompt versioning, eval pipelines, cost/latency monitoring, guardrails, tracing
Estimate the ongoing run cost and SLA staffing a productionised model demands, and place an org on an MLOps maturity ladder

subsections.title: The "Notebook → Product" Gap and the Production Loop

blocks.html:
A notebook proves a model can predict. A product requires that it predicts on live data, on time, every time, while someone watches it and pays the bill. The gap between those two is where most AI initiatives quietly die — not because the model was wrong, but because nobody owned everything around it.

The famous version of this: the ML code is the small box in the middle; the system around it is everything else.

blocks.text:
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
        │  data collection · validation · feature serving       │
        │  ┌──────────┐  serving · monitoring · CI/CD            │
        │  │ ML model │  retraining · registry · governance      │
        │  └──────────┘  infra · on-call · cost control          │
        └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
              the model is ~5-10% of the work that ships it

blocks.html: The other reframe a leader needs: production ML is a loop, not a launch. A model is never "done"; the world it learned from keeps moving.

blocks.text:
data ─▶ train ─▶ validate ─▶ deploy ─▶ serve ─▶ monitor ─┐
     ▲                                                       │
     └──────────────── retrain on drift / decay ◀────────────┘

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Shipping a model is taking on a liability, not banking an asset. The training project is a one-off cost; the loop above is a recurring one that runs until you decommission the model. Most failed AI programmes priced the launch and forgot the loop. When you scope, ask "who runs this in month seven?" — if there's no answer, you haven't finished scoping.

subsections.title: Serving Patterns: How Predictions Get Delivered

blocks.html: The single most cost- and complexity-determining choice is how predictions reach the consumer. Walk this table and stop at the cheapest pattern the use case tolerates — the same simplest-first discipline as AI & Data Landscape & Tech-Lead Mindset.

PatternLatencyWhen to reach for itRun cost & reliability tax
BatchHours–overnightPredictions can be precomputed (churn scores, nightly recommendations, risk lists)Lowest. A scheduled job; if it fails you rerun it. No always-on infra.
Real-time APIMillisecondsA user/system needs an answer now (fraud check, search rank, live quote)Always-on service, autoscaling, an SLA, on-call. The expensive default.
StreamingSecondsDecisions on a continuous event flow (clickstream, sensors, transactions)Highest plumbing cost: a streaming platform + state. Specialist skills.
Edge / on-deviceMilliseconds, offlineConnectivity, privacy, or latency forbid a round trip (mobile, IoT, vehicles)Hard updates, device fragmentation, no server-side fix. Niche, costly.

The decision in one line: default to batch; escalate to real-time only when a human or system genuinely cannot wait; reach for streaming/edge only when the use case structurally forces it. Teams routinely build a real-time API for predictions a nightly batch job would have served — and inherit an SLA, an on-call rota, and a multiple of the cost for nothing.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: "Real-time" is a cost decision dressed as a technical one. Going from batch to real-time often does not change the model at all — it changes your infra bill and your staffing, because always-on means someone is accountable when it falls over at 2am. Make the client name the cost of waiting before you agree they can't.

For the implementation — FastAPI handlers, batch scoring jobs, caching, feature serving, the full system-design framework — point to and reuse ML System Design and MLOps & Deployment. Do not rebuild it.

A leader needs the vocabulary, not the YAML. Three ideas cover most conversations:

  • Containers package the model with its exact environment (libraries, versions, code) so "works on my machine" becomes "works identically everywhere." This is what makes a deployment reproducible and is why "we'll just copy the notebook to the server" is a red flag.
  • APIs are the contract: a defined request in, a defined prediction out, versioned so consumers don't break when you update the model. The API is the product boundary — it's what the rest of the business integrates against.
  • Model registry is the versioned, governed store of which model is which: what data and code produced it, who approved it, whether it's in staging or production, and how to roll back. Without it you get model_final_v3_REAL.pkl and no audit trail (see Concept 6).

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Containers, an API contract, and a registry are the difference between a deployment you can reason about and one you merely hope about. They cost a few engineer-days up front and save the project the first time something breaks at 2am — which it will.
Dockerfiles, registry setup, and serving code live in MLOps & Deployment; do not re-teach them here — link.

subsections.title: Monitoring & Drift: Why Models Rot Silently

blocks.html:
Software fails loudly (a 500, a stack trace). Models fail silently: they keep returning confident, well-formed, increasingly wrong answers, and nothing throws an error. The world drifts away from the data the model learned, and only your business metrics — or a regulator — notice. This is the single most under-budgeted part of production ML.

Three distinct failure modes, often conflated:

Failure modeWhat changedPlain exampleFirst signal
Data driftThe inputs shiftedA new product line floods features the model never sawInput feature distributions move vs the training baseline
Concept driftThe input→output relationship shiftedCOVID changes what "normal" spend looks like; fraud tactics adaptAccuracy falls even though inputs look familiar
Model decayPerformance degrades over time, any causeQuietly worsening predictions, accumulatingThe live metric slides below its floor

What a tech lead must ensure is monitored — in rough priority:

  1. Live business / outcome metric (conversion, fraud caught, error cost). The only thing that actually matters; everything else is a leading indicator.
  2. Model quality metric once ground truth arrives — the same metric you validated on (tie this back to Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation; monitoring is just that evaluation, repeated forever on live data).
  3. Input drift — feature distributions vs the training baseline. Catches problems before the outcome does.
  4. Prediction drift — the distribution of outputs over time.
  5. Operational health — latency (P50/P95/P99), error rate, throughput, cost per call. The SLA layer.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Ground truth is usually late — you learn whether a churn prediction was right only weeks later. So you watch input/prediction drift as an early warning and treat the lagging outcome metric as the verdict. The trap is monitoring only latency and uptime (easy, technical) while the model's accuracy silently halves (hard, requires labels). A model can be 100% "up" and 100% wrong.
The headline number behind drift alerts is the Population Stability Index (PSI) — the metric most monitoring tools report when they tell you a feature has moved:
PSI=∑i(ai−ei) ln⁡aiei\text{PSI} = \sum_i (a_i - e_i)\,\ln\frac{a_i}{e_i}PSI=i∑​(ai​−ei​)lnei​ai​​

Bin the feature, then for each bin iii compare aia_iai​ — the actual proportion of live (production) records in that bin — against eie_iei​ — the expected proportion from the training baseline. The standard rule-of-thumb thresholds a leader should recognise on a dashboard:

PSIReadingAction
< 0.1StableNo action — the feature looks like training
0.1 – 0.25Moderate shiftWatch; investigate if several features move together
> 0.25Significant driftInvestigate and likely retrain — the model is seeing a different world

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: PSI is large exactly when much of the mass moved far: a bin contributes when its share changed (ai−eia_i - e_iai​−ei​) and the relative move is big (ln⁡aiei\ln\frac{a_i}{e_i}lnei​ai​​). A handful of records crossing a boundary barely registers; a whole segment vanishing or appearing lights it up. That is why it works as a single "how far has this feature drifted from training?" gauge you can threshold and alert on.

An alternative when you want a distribution-shape test rather than a binned score is the Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) statistic: D=max⁡x∣Fa(x)−Fe(x)∣D = \max_x \lvert F_a(x) - F_e(x)\rvertD=maxx​∣Fa​(x)−Fe​(x)∣ — the largest vertical gap between the actual and expected cumulative distributions. Bigger DDD = more drift; tools report it with a p-value per feature.

The mechanics of running these per feature are coded in MLOps & Deployment; the deeper statistics live in the AI Scientist Path. Your job is to insist the monitoring exists, has thresholds, and pages a human.

subsections.title: Retraining Strategy: Triggers and the Cost of Cadence

blocks.html: Once you accept production is a loop, the question becomes when do we close it — when do we retrain? Three triggers, and a cost trade behind each:

TriggerRetrain when…Watch out for
Drift / decay-triggeredMonitoring shows the live metric crossed its floor or inputs drifted materiallyBest signal, but needs the monitoring of Concept 4 to exist first
ScheduledA fixed cadence (nightly / weekly / monthly) regardless of signalSimple and predictable; retrains needlessly when stable, lags when volatile
Event-drivenA known change (new product, new market, policy shift, new labelled data)Requires the business to tell the ML system the world changed
The cadence trade is a genuine cost/benefit dial:

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Retraining isn't free and isn't always better. Each cycle costs compute, an engineer's time, and a fresh validation + re-approval (you cannot ship an unvalidated model — see Concept 7). Retrain too often and you burn money and risk shipping a worse model from a noisy week; too rarely and you eat the cost of decay. The right cadence is set by how fast this domain actually moves: fraud and pricing drift in days; a document classifier may be stable for a year. Tie cadence to observed drift, not to a calendar habit.

A pragmatic default for an early mission: monitor first, retrain manually on alert, automate the cadence only once you've seen how fast this model actually decays. Automated retraining is a capability to earn, not to ship on day one.

subsections.title: Experiment Tracking & Model Registry: Reproducibility as Governance

blocks.html:
Two adjacent capabilities a leader should treat as non-negotiable infrastructure, not nice-to-haves:

  • Experiment tracking records, for every training run, the parameters, metrics, code version, and data version that produced it. Without it, "which settings gave us that 0.92?" has no answer and the model is not reproducible — which is also a compliance failure.
  • Model registry (introduced in Concept 3) is the versioned, staged, approved store of production models, with lineage and rollback.

Together they answer the questions an auditor, a regulator, or a post-incident review will ask: which model was live on this date, what produced it, who approved it, and can we reproduce it? Under the EU AI Act this stops being good hygiene and becomes an obligation for higher-risk systems (see Responsible AI: Interpretability, Fairness, Safety & the EU AI Act).

The hands-on (MLflow / Weights & Biases setup, registry staging, A/B comparison) is in Experiment Tracking & Model Registry — point there; teach the why here, not the API.

subsections.title: CI/CD for ML: Same Spirit, Extra Gates

blocks.html: A leader who knows software CI/CD already has 80% of this. CI/CD for ML is ordinary continuous integration/deployment plus gates that software pipelines don't have, because an ML system can break in two ways software can't: bad data and a bad model — both of which pass every code test.

blocks.text:
Software CI/CD:   code ─▶ lint ─▶ unit tests ─▶ build ─▶ deploy
  ML CI/CD adds:                 ├▶ DATA validation  (schema, ranges, nulls)
                                 └▶ MODEL validation (metric above a floor on a
                                    held-out set, no regression vs production)
                                                      ─▶ deploy ─▶ monitor (Concept 4)

blocks.html:
The two extra gates are the whole point:

  • Data validation — the incoming/feature data matches the expected schema, ranges, and null rates. Catches the silent upstream breakage that would otherwise poison training or serving.
  • Model validation — the candidate clears a metric floor on a held-out set and doesn't regress against the model currently in production. This is the automated form of Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation, wired into the deploy pipeline so a worse model can't ship by accident.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: In software, the artefact is the code, and tests assert the code's behaviour. In ML, the artefact is code + data + a trained model, so you need to validate all three. A green test suite tells you the code runs; it tells you nothing about whether the model is any good or the data is sane. That's why "we have CI" doesn't mean "we have ML CI."

Putting a GenAI / LLM app in production rhymes with classic MLOps but adds problems that don't exist for a churn model. You usually don't own or retrain the model (you rent it via an API — see Large Language Models in Practice), so the operational weight shifts from training pipelines to prompts, evaluation, cost, latency, and guardrails.

What changes, and what a leader must put in place:

Classic MLOpsLLMOps addsWhy it matters
Version the modelVersion the prompt (and the model behind the API, which the vendor can change under you)A prompt edit is a deploy; an unannounced vendor model update is silent drift you don't control
Validate a metric on a labelled setEval pipelines — graded test suites (often LLM-as-judge + human spot-checks) for quality, safety, regressionThere's rarely a single accuracy number; you assemble a behavioural test suite (ties to Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation)
Cost ≈ infra timeToken cost & latency monitoring per call/featureSpend scales with usage and prompt length and can run away overnight — this is a live budget line, not a fixed cost
Input range checksGuardrails — input/output filtering, prompt-injection defence, PII redaction, refusal handlingThe failure mode is a confidently wrong, unsafe, or leaking answer to a user (ties to Agentic Architectures & LLM App Patterns)
Log predictionsTracing / observability — capture the full chain: prompt, retrieved context, tool calls, intermediate steps, final outputMulti-step RAG/agent apps are otherwise undebuggable; you can't fix what you can't see

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: With a classic model you mostly worry that it decays. With an LLM app you also worry that it misbehaves (hallucinates, gets jailbroken, leaks data) and that it costs an amount that moves with traffic. So LLMOps front-loads evals, guardrails, tracing, and a cost dashboard where classic MLOps front-loaded retraining pipelines. For agents specifically, tracing isn't optional — it's the only way to see why a five-step loop went wrong (see Agentic Architectures & LLM App Patterns).

subsections.title: Run Cost, SLA, and MLOps Maturity — the Budget Reality

blocks.html:
This is the section a tech lead is actually paid for. Three uncomfortable truths to put in front of a client:

  1. The run cost is recurring and often dwarfs the build. Compute (always-on serving, retraining, GPU/token spend), licences/tooling, and people (on-call, monitoring, retraining, incident response). A real-time model with an SLA is a permanent operational commitment, not a delivered artefact.

  2. An SLA is a staffing decision. "99.9% uptime" means someone is on-call and you've built failover. Promising an SLA you haven't staffed is the fastest route to a furious client. If nobody owns nights, you do not have an SLA — you have a hope.

    Availability is just uptime as a fraction of total time, quoted in "nines":

    Availability=uptimeuptime+downtime\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{uptime}}{\text{uptime} + \text{downtime}}Availability=uptime+downtimeuptime​

    Each extra nine costs roughly 10× the engineering. The numbers worth memorising — how much downtime a year each level permits:

    SLA"Nines"Allowed downtime / year
    99%"two nines"~3.65 days
    99.9%"three nines"~8.8 hours
    99.99%"four nines"~52 minutes

    The complement is the lever you actually manage — the error budget:

    Error budget=1−SLA\text{Error budget} = 1 - \text{SLA}Error budget=1−SLA

    Intuition. The error budget reframes reliability as a spendable allowance, not a moral absolute. A 99.9% SLA grants ~8.8 hours of failure a year; deploys, retrains, and experiments spend that budget. While budget remains you can take risks and ship; when it's exhausted you freeze changes and stabilise. It turns "don't break it" into a number both you and the client can plan against.

    The other reliability numbers a leader reads off a dashboard are latency percentiles — p50, p95, p99: the value below which 50%, 95%, or 99% of requests complete. p50 is the typical (median) request; p99 is the slow tail — 1 in 100 calls is at least this slow.

    Intuition. Report tail latency (p95/p99), not the average — the mean hides the pain. A snappy 40 ms average can still mean 1 request in 100 takes 3 seconds, and those slow calls land on your highest-volume users and trip your SLA. Users feel the worst experiences, not the typical one, so you size infra and write SLAs against the tail.

  3. Maturity is a ladder; don't sell a rung you can't stand on. Where an org sits determines what you can responsibly promise:

MaturityWhat it looks likeHonest promise at this level
0 — ManualNotebooks, manual deploys, no monitoring, "retrain when someone notices""We can ship one model, manually watched"
1 — RepeatableContainerised serving, experiment tracking, a registry, basic drift monitoring"Reproducible deploys and we'll see decay before the client does"
2 — AutomatedCI/CD with data+model gates, automated/triggered retraining, alerting"The loop largely runs itself; humans handle exceptions"
3 — Full MLOpsSelf-healing pipelines, feature store, A/B + canary, governance baked in"Fleet-scale, audited, multiple models — a platform"

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Most clients asking for "AI in production" are at level 0 and imagining level 3. Your value is naming the real rung, scoping the next one, and pricing the run cost they didn't see — not selling a maturity they can't operate. Selling level 3 to a level-0 org builds a platform nobody can run; the honest, sellable move is the uplift to the next rung.

Worked example — pricing the loop, not the launch

A retailer has a churn model from a PoC (notebook, ~0.88 AUC) and wants it "live so the CRM team gets daily at-risk scores."

  • Pattern: the CRM acts on a list each morning — batch, not real-time. That alone removes always-on infra, an SLA, and an on-call rota. (A team chasing novelty might have proposed a real-time API and 3× the run cost for zero added value.)
  • Build (one-off, ~4-6 weeks): containerise the model, a scheduled scoring job writing scores to the warehouse, a registry entry with lineage, a data-validation gate on the input feed, and drift + AUC monitoring with thresholds. The model is the smallest part.
  • Run (recurring — the part the client forgot): the nightly job's compute; monitoring + alerting; an estimated ~1-3 engineer-days/month to watch drift, investigate alerts, and retrain when the live AUC slips below an agreed floor (say 0.83). Quantify it as a monthly figure, because it never stops.
  • What you don't promise: a fixed accuracy forever (you commit to a floor and a response when breached), real-time latency, or "set and forget." You promise a monitored loop with a named owner and a retraining trigger.

The lesson is the whole module: the sellable, honest deliverable is the loop with its run cost made explicit, not a one-time "deployment."

These are the productionisation missions — they convert a promising PoC into something the client can actually rely on, and the run cost into a line item.

  • Productionize a PoC model (deploy + monitor) — Problem: a data-science PoC works in a notebook but isn't live and nobody trusts it to stay right. Deliverable: the model containerised and served in the right pattern (usually batch), with a registry entry, a data-validation gate, drift + performance monitoring with alert thresholds, and a written runbook naming the owner and retraining trigger. Scope: 4-8 weeks, fixed price. Who buys: a Head of Data / CTO who has a model and no way to operate it.
  • MLOps platform setup / maturity uplift — Problem: several teams ship models ad-hoc; no shared tooling, no reproducibility, deploys are heroic. Deliverable: a target-state MLOps architecture, a chosen and configured toolchain (registry, tracking, CI/CD with data+model gates, monitoring), reference templates, and an explicit move from maturity rung N to N+1. Scope: 6-12 weeks, often a retainer afterwards. Who buys: a platform/engineering lead or CTO scaling beyond the first model.
  • Monitoring & automated-retraining setup — Problem: a model is live but rotting blind, or retraining is a manual scramble. Deliverable: drift + outcome monitoring wired to alerts, a defined retraining trigger and cadence, and a validation-gated retraining pipeline so a worse model can't ship. Scope: 3-6 weeks. Who buys: the team already running a model in production who got burned by silent decay.
  • LLMOps for a GenAI application — Problem: a GenAI feature shipped on vibes — no prompt versioning, no evals, runaway token cost, no tracing, weak guardrails. Deliverable: prompt/version management, an automated eval suite (LLM-judge + human spot-checks), a cost & latency dashboard with budget alerts, guardrails (injection defence, PII redaction, refusal handling), and end-to-end tracing. Scope: 4-8 weeks. Who buys: a product owner whose GenAI feature is live and either overspending or misbehaving.

Buy-vs-build for the client: buy the commodity plumbing — registry, tracking, monitoring, tracing, serving runtimes are mature off-the-shelf products; do not build a bespoke MLOps platform for one or two models. Build only the glue, the validation gates, and the domain-specific evals — that's where the defensible value is. What to charge for: the build as fixed price, and the run/operate loop as a retainer or managed-service line — clients underprice this, so make it explicit and recurring. What NOT to promise: a permanent accuracy number (commit to a floor + a response), an SLA you haven't staffed, "fully autonomous retraining" on a green-field engagement, or a maturity rung the client's team cannot operate after you leave.
As of 2026 — treat names/prices as perishable; re-check before quoting.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote (lock-in / caveat)
MLflowExperiment tracking + model registryOSS (managed options exist)The common open default; self-host to avoid lock-in
Weights & BiasesTracking, registry, dashboardsPaid (free tier)Polished; per-seat cost, hosted by default
Evidently AI · NannyMLDrift & performance monitoringOSS (paid cloud)Pairs with your own pipeline; NannyML estimates performance without labels
Cloud ML platforms (SageMaker, Vertex AI, Azure ML)End-to-end lifecycle on one cloudPaid (consumption)Fast to start; the strongest lock-in — watch egress & buy-in
KServe · Seldon · BentoMLModel serving runtimesOSS (paid tiers)For real-time/multi-model scale; Kubernetes skills assumed
Feature stores (Feast, Tecton)Consistent features train↔serveOSS / PaidReal value only past a certain scale — overkill for a first model
LangSmith · Langfuse · Arize PhoenixLLM/agent tracing, evals, observabilityPaid / OSSThe LLMOps tracing layer; Langfuse/Phoenix are OSS options
Helicone · vendor usage dashboardsLLM cost & latency monitoringPaid (free tier)Watch token spend per feature; budget alerts essential
GitHub Actions · GitLab CI (+ data/model gates)CI/CD with ML validation gatesPaid / OSSReuse existing software CI; add the data+model gates yourself
For how to wire these up, the ML Engineer Path is the territory; this table is just the map.

SituationFull MLOps is overkill — do this instead
One model, scored monthly, by one analystA scheduled script + a logged metric. Skip the platform.
A genuine PoC still proving valueDon't productionise yet — keep it a notebook until the value is proven; productionising a doomed PoC is wasted money.
A single low-volume LLM feature, internal onlyVersion prompts in git, a lightweight eval checklist, a cost alert. Skip the full LLMOps stack.
Predictions tolerate overnight stalenessBatch. Don't build a real-time API and inherit an SLA.
Two or three models totalBuy a managed registry + monitoring tool. Don't build a bespoke platform.
Stable domain, model decays yearlyMonitor + retrain manually on alert. Don't automate a retraining pipeline you'll run once a year.
The senior move is matching the operational machinery to the number and volatility of models, not to ambition. A platform for one stable model is a cost centre with no payoff.

• Chip Huyen — Designing Machine Learning Systems — the canonical text; deployment, monitoring, and the production loop in business-aware language. (Paid)
  • Chip Huyen — AI Engineering — the LLMOps-era companion: evals, prompts, cost, guardrails for GenAI apps. (Paid)

• MLOps Zoomcamp (DataTalksClub) — end-to-end, hands-on; good for the team that will operate the loop.
  • Full Stack Deep Learning — Deployment & Monitoring — pragmatic lectures on shipping and watching models.

• Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems (Sculley et al.) — why the model is the small box; foundational reading for the run-cost argument.
  • Continuous Delivery for Machine Learning (Sato/Fowler, martinfowler.com) — CI/CD-for-ML in clear, leader-readable terms.
  • Vendor LLM pricing pages — for live token cost when budgeting an LLMOps mission. Date everything.

• Deeper systems & technical leadership → ML Systems Engineering & Technical Leadership (AI Scientist Path).
  • Deeper code: serving, Docker, CI/CD, drift → MLOps & Deployment; reproducibility & registries → Experiment Tracking & Model Registry; end-to-end architecture → ML System Design (ML Engineer Path).

subsections.title: Decision Checklist — "Is this model ready for production?"

items.html: Is the serving pattern the cheapest the use case tolerates (defaulted to batch, escalated only with a reason)?
Is the model containerised and registered with lineage (data + code version), not a loose .pkl?
Is there a data-validation gate on the inputs and a model-validation gate (metric floor, no regression) before deploy?
Is monitoring live for the outcome metric and input drift — not just latency/uptime — with thresholds that alert a human?
Is there a named retraining trigger and an owner who runs this in month seven?
For LLM apps: prompts versioned, an eval suite, a cost/latency alert, guardrails, and tracing?
Has the recurring run cost (compute + tooling + people) been quantified and priced — not just the build?

blocks.html:
• "We'll just copy the notebook to the server." (No container, no reproducibility, no gates.)
  • "It's deployed, so we're done." (You've started the loop, not finished the project.)
  • Monitoring shows uptime and latency but no model-quality or drift metric. (Up and wrong.)
  • A real-time API for predictions a nightly batch job would serve. (Self-inflicted SLA and cost.)
  • An SLA promised with nobody on-call. (A hope, not an SLA.)
  • A bespoke MLOps platform proposed for one or two models. (Buy it.)
  • A live GenAI feature with no prompt versioning, no evals, and no cost alert. (Misbehaving and overspending, blind.)
  • "It'll retrain itself automatically" on a green-field, level-0 engagement. (Earned, not shipped on day one.)

• "Who operates and watches this model in six months, and is that role staffed?"
  • "How fresh do predictions need to be — is overnight acceptable, or must it be instant? What's the cost of waiting?"
  • "How will we know the model has gone wrong, and how fast does ground truth arrive?"
  • "How quickly does this domain change — what's actually driving a retrain?"
  • "What uptime do you truly need, and have we staffed the on-call that implies?"
  • "What's the monthly run budget — compute, tooling, and people — and who owns it?"
  • (GenAI) "What's the per-feature token budget, what happens when the model is unsafe or wrong, and can we trace why?"
Before moving on, you should be able to:

items.html: Explain the notebook→product gap and frame production ML as a recurring loop, not a launch
Choose a serving pattern from a use case and state its run-cost and reliability consequences
Specify what to monitor so a silently rotting model is caught, tying it back to evaluation
Set and defend a retraining cadence on cost-vs-decay grounds
Articulate how ML CI/CD differs from software CI/CD and why the data+model gates matter
Scope an LLMOps engagement (prompts, evals, cost/latency, guardrails, tracing) and place a client on the MLOps maturity ladder with an honest next-rung promise
